steps.voice: Long-press any widget on the dashboard — about half a second — and its config modal pops open. No menu hunting, no separate edit mode. Same gesture works on every widget type the dash supports: panels, bars, meters, indicators, RPM bars, the lot.
Three tabs across the top — CAN Signal, Display, and Alerts. Everything you can change about this one widget lives behind those three.

Display tab first — the easy stuff. Label text, font family, decimal places, unit suffix, colours, and any widget-specific knobs like a redline value on the RPM bar. Editing here doesn't touch the CAN side at all, so you can rename or recolour without breaking the data binding.

Data Source picker — this is where the widget gets bound to a CAN signal. Pick from the ECU preset dropdowns: brand, then version, then channel. Haltech Nexus has the full Elite stream; MaxxECU v1.3 has 100-plus channels; Ford BA / BF / FG covers the core powertrain. Hit Apply and the widget starts updating with live data.

ECU not in the presets? Expand CAN Bus Settings and enter the signal by hand — CAN ID in hex, bit start (zero to sixty-three), bit length, scale, offset, and endianness. The displayed value is just raw times scale plus offset. If a value reads sixteen-thousand instead of sixty-four, you've almost certainly got endianness flipped — toggle Intel to Motorola and it'll snap right.

Alerts tab covers the warning thresholds — set a low and high band per signal, pick the alert colour. The widget flashes when the value crosses either threshold, and the same trigger can fire a warning lamp anywhere on the layout.
Cancel discards everything. Save writes to LittleFS and reloads the layout in-place — about a one-second debounce, so the dash never feels frozen while you tweak.
